chunks.title: Fuzzy clustering
chunks.content: The slide introduces the concept of fuzzy c-means clustering, explaining the fuzzy pseudopartition or fuzzy c-partition of a dataset. It provides mathematical conditions that fuzzy subsets must satisfy for a valid fuzzy partition.
chunks.raw_text:
Fuzzy clustering

Fuzzy c-means clustering method

Let X = {x_1, x_2, ..., x_n} be a set of given data. A fuzzy pseudopartition or fuzzy c-partition of X is a family of fuzzy subsets of X, denoted by P = {A_1, A_2, ..., A_c}, which satisfies

Σ A_i(x_k) = 1 for all k ∈ N_n and

0 < Σ A_i(x_k) < n for all i ∈ N_c, where c is a positive integer.
chunks.embedding_text: The slide introduces the concept of fuzzy c-means clustering, explaining the fuzzy pseudopartition or fuzzy c-partition of a dataset. It provides mathematical conditions that fuzzy subsets must satisfy for a valid fuzzy partition.

chunks.content: A method used to partition a set of data into fuzzy subsets, where each data point can belong to multiple clusters with varying degrees of membership.
chunks.embedding_text: Fuzzy c-means clustering method: A method used to partition a set of data into fuzzy subsets, where each data point can belong to multiple clusters with varying degrees of membership.

chunks.content: A family of fuzzy subsets of a dataset that satisfies specific mathematical conditions for membership values.
chunks.embedding_text: Fuzzy pseudopartition (or fuzzy c-partition): A family of fuzzy subsets of a dataset that satisfies specific mathematical conditions for membership values.

formula.description: The sum of membership values for each data point x_k across all clusters equals 1.

embedding_text.description: The sum of membership values for each data point x_k across all clusters equals 1.

formula.description: The sum of membership values for each cluster across all data points is strictly between 0 and the number of data points n.

embedding_text.description: The sum of membership values for each cluster across all data points is strictly between 0 and the number of data points n.

chunks.title: Fuzzy clustering
chunks.content: The slide explains how to calculate cluster centers in fuzzy clustering using a pseudopartition and a formula involving membership grades raised to a power m, which influences the clustering process.
chunks.raw_text:
Fuzzy clustering

Given a pseudopartition P = {A1, A2, ..., Ac}, the c cluster centers, v1, v2, ..., vc associated with the partition are calculated by the formula

v_i = (Σ[from k=1 to n] [A_i(x_k)]^m * x_k) / (Σ[from k=1 to n] [A_i(x_k)]^m)

for all i ∈ Nc, where m > 1 is a real number that governs the influence of membership grades.
chunks.embedding_text: The slide explains how to calculate cluster centers in fuzzy clustering using a pseudopartition and a formula involving membership grades raised to a power m, which influences the clustering process.

chunks.embedding_text: Pseudopartition: A collection of fuzzy subsets {A1, A2, ..., Ac} representing clusters in fuzzy clustering.

chunks.title: Fuzzy clustering
chunks.content: This slide explains Step 4 of an iterative algorithm for fuzzy clustering, focusing on the convergence criterion. It describes how to compare successive partitions and decide whether to stop the iteration based on a threshold distance.
chunks.raw_text:
Fuzzy clustering

Step 4. Compare P^(t) and P^(t+1). If |P^(t+1) - P^(t)| ≤ ε, then stop; otherwise, increase t by one and return to Step 2.

In Step 4, |P^(t+1) - P^(t)| denotes a distance between P^(t+1) and P^(t) in the space ℝ^(n×c).
chunks.embedding_text: This slide explains Step 4 of an iterative algorithm for fuzzy clustering, focusing on the convergence criterion. It describes how to compare successive partitions and decide whether to stop the iteration based on a threshold distance.

chunks.title: Fuzzy clustering
chunks.content: The slide explains the concept of fuzzy clustering by equivalence, focusing on identifying an appropriate fuzzy equivalence relation on given data. It describes the process of determining a fuzzy compatibility relation using a distance function and then deriving a meaningful fuzzy equivalence relation through the transitive closure of this compatibility relation.
chunks.raw_text:
Fuzzy clustering

► Clustering by Equivalence
The fuzzy clustering problem can be viewed as the problem of identifying an appropriate fuzzy equivalence relation on given data.
It usually cannot be done directly, we can readily determine a fuzzy compatibility relation (reflexive and symmetric) in terms of an appropriate distance function applied to given data.
Then, a meaningful fuzzy equivalence relation is defined as the transitive closure of this fuzzy compatibility relation.
chunks.embedding_text: The slide explains the concept of fuzzy clustering by equivalence, focusing on identifying an appropriate fuzzy equivalence relation on given data. It describes the process of determining a fuzzy compatibility relation using a distance function and then deriving a meaningful fuzzy equivalence relation through the transitive closure of this compatibility relation.

chunks.embedding_text: Fuzzy compatibility relation: A reflexive and symmetric relation determined by an appropriate distance function applied to given data.

chunks.content: A meaningful relation defined as the transitive closure of a fuzzy compatibility relation.
chunks.embedding_text: Fuzzy equivalence relation: A meaningful relation defined as the transitive closure of a fuzzy compatibility relation.

chunks.title: Fuzzy clustering
chunks.content: The slide explains how to define a fuzzy compatibility relation using a Minkowski distance function for a dataset of p-tuples in ℝ^p. It introduces the formula for the fuzzy relation R(x_i, x_k) and explains the role of the parameter δ in ensuring the relation values lie within the interval [0, 1].
chunks.raw_text:
Fuzzy clustering

Distance function

Given a set of data  X  of the same form as defined earlier in this section  (n  p-tuples of ℝ^p), let a fuzzy compatibility relation, R, on X be defined in terms of an appropriate distance function of the Minkowski class by the formula

R(x_i, x_k) = 1 - δ(Σ_{j=1}^p |x_ij - x_kj|^q)^{1/q}

for all pairs  ⟨x_i, x_k⟩ ∈ X, where  q ∈ ℝ^+, and  δ  is a constant that ensures that  R(x_i, x_k) ∈ [0, 1]. Clearly, δ is the inverse value of the largest distance in X.
chunks.embedding_text: The slide explains how to define a fuzzy compatibility relation using a Minkowski distance function for a dataset of p-tuples in ℝ^p. It introduces the formula for the fuzzy relation R(x_i, x_k) and explains the role of the parameter δ in ensuring the relation values lie within the interval [0, 1].

chunks.content: A relation defined on a set of data X using a distance function of the Minkowski class, ensuring values are within the range [0, 1].
chunks.embedding_text: Fuzzy compatibility relation, R: A relation defined on a set of data X using a distance function of the Minkowski class, ensuring values are within the range [0, 1].

chunks.content: A constant that ensures the fuzzy compatibility relation R(x_i, x_k) values lie within [0, 1], specifically the inverse of the largest distance in the dataset X.
chunks.embedding_text: δ (delta): A constant that ensures the fuzzy compatibility relation R(x_i, x_k) values lie within [0, 1], specifically the inverse of the largest distance in the dataset X.

chunks.title: Fuzzy clustering
chunks.content: The slide presents Theorem 10.1, which discusses the max-min transitive closure of a fuzzy compatibility relation on a finite universal set. It states that the max-min transitive closure of a fuzzy compatibility relation R on a set X with n elements is given by the relation R^(n-1).
chunks.raw_text:
Fuzzy clustering

Theorem 10.1

Let R be a fuzzy compatibility relation on a finite universal set X with |X| = n. Then, the max-min transitive closure of R is the relation R^(n-1).
chunks.embedding_text: The slide presents Theorem 10.1, which discusses the max-min transitive closure of a fuzzy compatibility relation on a finite universal set. It states that the max-min transitive closure of a fuzzy compatibility relation R on a set X with n elements is given by the relation R^(n-1).

chunks.content: A relation R on a set X where elements have degrees of compatibility rather than binary yes/no relations.
chunks.embedding_text: Fuzzy compatibility relation: A relation R on a set X where elements have degrees of compatibility rather than binary yes/no relations.

chunks.content: A method to achieve transitivity in fuzzy relations by iteratively applying max-min composition until no further changes occur.
chunks.embedding_text: Max-min transitive closure: A method to achieve transitivity in fuzzy relations by iteratively applying max-min composition until no further changes occur.

chunks.content: Understanding the concept of fuzzy compatibility relations and their properties.

chunks.title: Fuzzy clustering
chunks.content: The slide illustrates the fuzzy clustering method using a small dataset in ℝ². It demonstrates how to determine fuzzy equivalence relations by analyzing the effect of the parameter q in the membership function. The example uses q = 2 (Euclidean distance) and explains the calculation of membership grades using a given formula.
chunks.raw_text:
Fuzzy clustering

Example 10.2
To illustrate the clustering method based on fuzzy equivalence relations, let us use a small data set X consisting of the following five points in ℝ²:

k | 1  2  3  4  5
x_k1 | 0  1  2  3  4
x_k2 | 0  1  3  1  0

The data are also shown in Fig. 10.2a. To see the effect of the value of parameter q in (10.6) on the results, let us analyze the data for q = 1, 2.

As the first step, we perform the analysis for q = 2, which corresponds to the Euclidean distance. First, we need to determine the value of δ in (10.6). Since the largest Euclidean distance between any pair of the given data points is 4 (between x1 and x5), we have δ = 1/4 = 0.25. Now, we can calculate membership grades of R by (10.6). For example,

R(x1, x3) = 1 - 0.25((2^2 + 3^2)^0.5) = 0.1.
chunks.embedding_text: The slide illustrates the fuzzy clustering method using a small dataset in ℝ². It demonstrates how to determine fuzzy equivalence relations by analyzing the effect of the parameter q in the membership function. The example uses q = 2 (Euclidean distance) and explains the calculation of membership grades using a given formula.

chunks.title: Fuzzy clustering
chunks.content: The slide explains the application of fuzzy clustering using the Hamming distance for q = 1. It demonstrates how to derive a fuzzy relation matrix R and its transitive closure R_T, and how these matrices yield different partitions at various alpha-cut levels. The slide also compares the results with visual perception and discusses the limitations of using Hamming distance for clustering.
chunks.raw_text:
Fuzzy clustering

Let us now repeat the analysis for q = 1 in (10.6), which represents the Hamming distance. Since the largest Hamming distance in the data is 5 (between x1 and x3 and between x3 and x5), we have δ = 1/5 = 0.2. The matrix form of relation R given by (10.6) is now

R = [1  .6  0  .2  .2; .6  1  .4  .6  .2; 0  .4  1  .4  0; .2  .6  .4  1  .6; .2  .2  0  .6  1]

and its transitive closure is

R_T = [1  .6  .4  .6  .6; .6  1  .4  .6  .6; .4  .4  1  .4  .4; .6  .6  .4  1  .6; .6  .6  .4  .6  1]

This relation yields the following partitions in its α-cuts:

α ∈ [0, .4] : {{x1, x2, x3, x4, x5}},
α ∈ (.4, .6] : {{x1, x2, x4, x5}, {x3}},
α ∈ (.6, 1] : {{x1}, {x2}, {x3}, {x4}, {x5}}.

This result again agrees with our visual perception, but it is less refined, since visually perceived clusters {x1, x2} and {x4, x5} are not distinguished. This is due to the coarseness of the Hamming distance. Both results are illustrated in Fig. 10.2 by their clustering trees, which are self-explanatory.
chunks.embedding_text: The slide explains the application of fuzzy clustering using the Hamming distance for q = 1. It demonstrates how to derive a fuzzy relation matrix R and its transitive closure R_T, and how these matrices yield different partitions at various alpha-cut levels. The slide also compares the results with visual perception and discusses the limitations of using Hamming distance for clustering.

chunks.embedding_text: Hamming distance: A measure of the difference between two strings of equal length, which is the number of positions at which the corresponding symbols are different.

chunks.content: A matrix that includes all transitive relations derived from the original relation matrix.
chunks.embedding_text: Transitive closure (R_T): A matrix that includes all transitive relations derived from the original relation matrix.

chunks.title: Fuzzy image processing
chunks.content: The slide explains the conversion of a standard image matrix into a fuzzy matrix using a specific formula. It introduces the concept of fuzzy image processing and the parameters involved in the conversion process.
chunks.raw_text:
Fuzzy image processing

► Fuzzy matrix

In fuzzy image-processing methods, matrix R = [r_ij] of a given image is converted into its fuzzy counterpart, R̃ = [r̃_ij], which is then manipulated by appropriate fuzzy operators. The conversion from R to R̃ is usually done by the formula

r̃_ij = (1 + (b̂ - r_ij) / β)^(-γ) (10.11)

for all i ∈ N_m and j ∈ N_n, where b̂ ∈ [0, b_max] is a reference constant defining the degree of brightness for which r̃_ij = 1, and β, γ are positive parameters that affect the conversion formula and are determined from required properties of enhancement operations to be applied to a given image.
chunks.embedding_text: The slide explains the conversion of a standard image matrix into a fuzzy matrix using a specific formula. It introduces the concept of fuzzy image processing and the parameters involved in the conversion process.

chunks.content: A matrix representation of an image where each element is converted to reflect fuzzy membership values.
chunks.embedding_text: Fuzzy matrix: A matrix representation of an image where each element is converted to reflect fuzzy membership values.

chunks.content: A constant within the range [0, b_max] defining the degree of brightness for which the fuzzy matrix element equals 1.
chunks.embedding_text: Reference constant (b̂): A constant within the range [0, b_max] defining the degree of brightness for which the fuzzy matrix element equals 1.

chunks.content: Positive parameters affecting the conversion formula, determined based on required properties of enhancement operations.
chunks.embedding_text: Parameters (β, γ): Positive parameters affecting the conversion formula, determined based on required properties of enhancement operations.

embedding_text.description: Conversion formula from a standard image matrix R to its fuzzy counterpart R̃.

chunks.title: Fuzzy image processing
chunks.content: The slide explains the process of converting a discrete image of handwritten letters into a fuzzy representation using an S-shaped membership function. It also describes the concept of contrast intensification operations for enhancing image quality for pattern recognition and classification.
chunks.raw_text:
Fuzzy image processing

The image considered in this example is the discrete approximation of a picture of three handwritten letters, Shu, by a 96 × 96 spatial grid of points and 32 levels of brightness (grey levels), shown in Fig. 10.8a. The image is represented by a 96 × 96 matrix R = [r_ij], where r_ij ∈ {0, 1, ..., 31} for all i, j ∈ N_96. For converting R into R̃, b̂ = b_max = 31 is chosen in this example and, hence,

r̃_ij = (1 + (31 - r_ij)/β)^(-γ),

where appropriate values of parameters β and γ are selected on the basis of the requirements imposed upon the following contrast intensification operation, which is one of the operations intended to enhance the image for subsequent pattern recognition and classification. Observe that this conversion function is an S-shape function, since it is based on b̂ = b_max = 31.

Any contrast intensification operation, INT, is a function of the form

INT : [0, 1] → [0, 1]

such that

INT(a) > a when a ∈ (0.5, 1),
INT(a) < a when a ∈ (0, 0.5),
INT(a) = a when a = 0, 0.5, 1.
chunks.embedding_text: The slide explains the process of converting a discrete image of handwritten letters into a fuzzy representation using an S-shaped membership function. It also describes the concept of contrast intensification operations for enhancing image quality for pattern recognition and classification.

chunks.content: Representation of an image by a 96 x 96 spatial grid of points with 32 levels of brightness.
chunks.embedding_text: Discrete approximation of an image: Representation of an image by a 96 x 96 spatial grid of points with 32 levels of brightness.

chunks.content: A function that enhances the contrast of an image by increasing intensity values in the range (0.5, 1) and decreasing those in the range (0, 0.5), leaving 0, 0.5, and 1 unchanged.
chunks.embedding_text: Contrast Intensification (INT): A function that enhances the contrast of an image by increasing intensity values in the range (0.5, 1) and decreasing those in the range (0, 0.5), leaving 0, 0.5, and 1 unchanged.

chunks.content: A type of membership function used to convert crisp values into fuzzy values, based on a maximum brightness level.
chunks.embedding_text: S-shape function: A type of membership function used to convert crisp values into fuzzy values, based on a maximum brightness level.

formula.description: Conversion formula for transforming the original image matrix R into a fuzzy matrix R̃.

embedding_text.description: Conversion formula for transforming the original image matrix R into a fuzzy matrix R̃.

chunks.content: Understanding the conversion of a discrete image into a fuzzy representation using an S-shape function.

chunks.content: The role of contrast intensification in image processing.
chunks.embedding_text: The role of contrast intensification in image processing.

chunks.title: Fuzzy image processing
chunks.content: The slide explains the concept of an intensification operation (INT) applied to entries of a matrix in fuzzy image processing. It defines the mathematical function INT and discusses the selection of an equilibrium point (b_c) for the conversion function to ensure intensification behavior. It also provides an example of determining parameters β and γ for a specific subinterval.
chunks.raw_text:
Fuzzy image processing

When function INT is applied to entries of matrix R̃, let INT(r̃_ij) be denoted by r̃'_ij. The intensification operation employed in this example is defined by

INT(r̃_ij) = { 2r̃_ij^2, when r̃_ij ∈ [0, 0.5]; 1 - 2(1 - r̃_ij)^2, when r̃_ij ∈ [0.5, 1] }

The intensification operation INT requires selection of a suitable equilibrium point, b_c, of the conversion function to ensure that r̃'_ij > r̃_ij for r̃_ij > b_c and r̃'_ij < r̃_ij for r̃_ij < b_c. By choosing a subinterval of [0, b_max] within which we want to apply the intensification operation, we can determine values of β and γ in the conversion function that satisfy this requirement. Assume, for example, that we want to apply the intensification operation within the subinterval [x, x + 1] of [0, b_max]. Then b_c = x + 0.5 and

(1 + (31 - b_c)/β)^(-γ) = 0.5.

Considering, for example, x = 9 and choosing γ = 1, we obtain

(1 + 21.5/β)^(-1) = 0.5,

which yields β = 21.5. Similarly, choosing γ = 2, we obtain β = 52, and so on.
chunks.embedding_text: The slide explains the concept of an intensification operation (INT) applied to entries of a matrix in fuzzy image processing. It defines the mathematical function INT and discusses the selection of an equilibrium point (b_c) for the conversion function to ensure intensification behavior. It also provides an example of determining parameters β and γ for a specific subinterval.

chunks.content: A function applied to entries of a matrix to enhance or reduce pixel intensities based on a defined equilibrium point.

chunks.title: Fuzzy image processing
chunks.content: The slide explains the application of intensification and smoothing operations in fuzzy image processing. It demonstrates how the intensification operator can be applied repeatedly to enhance image contrast and how smoothing can equalize brightness levels in neighboring pixels to produce a clearer image for pattern recognition and classification.
chunks.raw_text:
Fuzzy image processing

The intensification operator may be applied repeatedly. When it is applied twice to the image in Fig. 10.8a for β = 43 and γ = 2, we obtain the image in Fig. 10.8b.

The second operation on fuzzified images to be illustrated by this example is smoothing. The purpose of smoothing is to equalize the level of brightness of image points that are spatially close to each other. There are several known smoothing operations (or algorithms). One of them is based on averaging the brightness levels within neighbors. Assuming the use of the arithmetic average, the smoothing operation is defined by the formula

r''_uv = (1/4) Σ r'_ij for (i,j) ∈ Q

for all u ∈ N_m and v ∈ N_n, where Q = {(u, v+1), (u, v-1), (u+1, v), (u-1, v)} is the neighborhood of pixel (u, v). Applying this operation to the image in Fig. 13.8b, we obtain the smoother image in Fig. 10.8c.

Comparing the three images in Fig. 10.8, we can clearly see the effect of the two operations illustrated, contrast stretching (intensification) and smoothing (averaging). After the original image is processed by these two operations, it is undoubtedly more suitable for the subsequent pattern recognition and classification.
chunks.embedding_text: The slide explains the application of intensification and smoothing operations in fuzzy image processing. It demonstrates how the intensification operator can be applied repeatedly to enhance image contrast and how smoothing can equalize brightness levels in neighboring pixels to produce a clearer image for pattern recognition and classification.
